summary: O'Neil documents her attempt to obtain the source code for New York's teacher Value-Added Model through a FOIL request, only to be stonewalled with bureaucratic delays and ultimately sent a white paper instead of the actual code she requested. After months of waiting, the Department of Education responded with exactly what she had explicitly said she didn't want. She argues that without access to the underlying code, teachers are being judged by a secret system with enormous power over their careers, while their individual scores have already been publicly released and used to shame them.
key_points: FOIL requests for algorithmic source code are met with bureaucratic obstruction and deliberate misunderstanding
Teachers' VAM scores were publicly released via FOIA, but the algorithm that produces those scores remains hidden
White papers describing models are insufficient for understanding actual model behavior and sensitivity to inputs
Algorithms with significant power over people's lives operate as 'secret sets of rules' without accountability
Three months of delays resulted in receiving exactly what was explicitly not requested
money_quotes: Without knowledge of what the model consists of, the VAM scoring regime is little more than a secret set of rules, with tremendous power over teachers and the teacher union.
I think teachers deserve better, and I want to illustrate the weaknesses of the model directly.
It strikes me as strange that it took them more than 3 months to send me a link to a white paper instead of the source code as I requested.
The best way to get at that is the source code.
key_insight: When institutions publicly release algorithmic scores that damage individuals' reputations while refusing to release the code that generates those scores, they create an accountability asymmetry that protects the powerful and exposes the vulnerable.
